visual_checks: Look for a site-wide navigation menu that allows users to reach this page from other pages in the site
Check if the site provides a search function (search box, search page) that can be used to find this page's content
Look for a site map page that lists all pages or major sections of the site
Check for a table of contents on long pages or within multi-page documents
Look for breadcrumb navigation that shows the page's position in the site hierarchy
Verify that the page is reachable through at least two distinct navigation mechanisms (e.g., main nav plus search, or main nav plus site map)
pass_conditions: The page can be reached through at least two different mechanisms from the following: navigation menu, search function, site map, table of contents, links from related pages, breadcrumb trail, A-Z index
A site search function is available and functional across the site
A site map or similar comprehensive listing of pages is available
Navigation menus provide consistent access to major site sections
Breadcrumb navigation or hierarchical links allow users to navigate to the page's position in the site structure
fail_conditions: The page can only be reached through a single mechanism (e.g., only through the main navigation, with no search or site map)
No search function is provided and the only way to reach pages is through the navigation menu
The site has no site map and no search function and relies solely on hierarchical menu navigation
Pages are reachable only through specific sequences of interactions with no alternative path
na_conditions: The page is part of or the result of a multi-step process (e.g., a checkout confirmation page, a form submission result) where providing multiple ways to reach it would disrupt the process
The page is a single standalone page with no parent site structure
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Cannot determine all available navigation mechanisms from a single page view — site-wide testing required
Search functionality cannot be tested from static screenshots
common_mistakes: Providing only a navigation menu with no search function or site map on large sites
Having a search function that does not index all pages, making some content unreachable via search
Site map exists but is outdated and does not include recently added pages
Breadcrumb navigation is present but does not link to parent pages (text-only breadcrumbs)
Search function exists but is broken or returns no results for valid queries
Relying solely on JavaScript-driven navigation with no fallback for users who navigate differently

examples.pass: The site provides a main navigation menu with dropdown sub-menus, a search box in the header that searches all site content, a site map page linked from the footer, and breadcrumb navigation on every interior page
examples.fail: A large corporate website has a hierarchical navigation menu as the only way to find pages — there is no search function, no site map, and no breadcrumb navigation, so users must navigate the menu tree to find any page
examples.partial: The site has a main navigation menu and a search function, but the search only indexes blog posts and does not return results for important pages like the contact page, about page, or product pages

plain_meaning: Within a set of related web pages, users must have more than one way to find any given page. Typical mechanisms include a site-wide navigation menu, a search feature, a site map, a table of contents, or a list of related pages. The exception is pages that are a step in a process such as a checkout, where skipping around would break the flow.
scope_applies_to: Multi-page websites and sets of related pages
Primary navigation menus listing sections of the site
Tables of contents and related-page links
Breadcrumb trails when they also help users locate pages
scope_does_not_apply_to: Single-page applications or isolated one-off pages
Pages that are a required step in a linear process (checkout, wizard, legal agreement flow)
The visual design or styling of navigation
Whether navigation is programmatically labeled as a landmark (that is SC 1.3.1)
Whether navigation is keyboard operable (that is SC 2.1.1)
Whether link purposes are clear (that is SC 2.4.4)
borderline_calibration: Two mechanisms is enough; do not require three.
A working site search plus a persistent nav menu satisfies this SC.
Only flag when the page under test truly has no second way to be located.
Do not fail a checkout step page or a similar process step.
Do not fail a standalone page that is not part of a set.
